matches.match_rationale: The network lacks unified employer-facing materials — each campus builds employer relationships independently with inconsistent messaging. Text generation can produce standardized employer engagement collateral (pitch decks, capability statements, graduate profiles) that present the network as a single credentialed brand. It can also generate templated outreach emails, alumni newsletters, and placement reports that create consistent employer touchpoints across all campuses.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An AI-assisted content system that generates unified employer engagement materials and alumni communications from a shared brand framework, replacing the patchwork of campus-specific outreach documents.

matches.match_rationale: Job placement rates vary widely and are inconsistently tracked across campuses, making it impossible to present a coherent network-level employment story to employers. Summarization can condense scattered placement data, employer feedback, and graduate outcome reports into unified network-level summaries that make the combined graduate base legible to external parties. This directly addresses the employer perception problem by creating digestible evidence of network-wide quality.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated reporting tool that aggregates campus-level placement data and employer feedback into a single network outcomes summary, updated periodically and shareable with prospective employer partners.

matches.match_rationale: Each campus has siloed employer relationships with no visibility into the network's combined employer graph. Knowledge extraction can map the relationships between campuses, employers, alumni, and industry sectors — surfacing which employers already work with multiple campuses, which alumni are in influential hiring roles, and where relationship gaps exist. This structured knowledge graph becomes the foundation for a coordinated employer engagement strategy.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A relationship intelligence layer that ingests alumni records, employer contacts, and placement history across campuses to build a unified network graph, revealing untapped shared employer relationships and alumni influence nodes.

matches.match_rationale: Job placement rates vary widely and are inconsistently tracked, partly because outcomes are categorized differently across campuses. Structured data classification can normalize graduate employment records — standardizing job titles, industry sectors, and employment types — enabling apples-to-apples comparison and a coherent network-wide placement metric that employers and leadership can trust. This addresses the quality variance perception problem with evidence.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A data standardization pipeline that classifies and normalizes graduate employment outcomes across campuses into a consistent taxonomy, producing a unified placement rate dashboard for employer-facing and leadership reporting.

matches.match_rationale: Employers approaching the network have no reliable way to understand what graduates from specific campuses know and can do. A RAG system can answer employer queries — 'What do your graduates know about X?', 'Which campus has the strongest program in Y?' — grounded in curriculum documents, graduate profiles, and placement records. This creates a consistent, accurate employer-facing interface that replaces fragmented campus-by-campus conversations.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An employer-facing Q&A portal powered by RAG over the network's curriculum, graduate competency, and placement data — allowing recruiters to query the combined graduate base and get sourced, campus-specific or network-wide answers.

matches.match_rationale: With no unified alumni network or career services, graduates and employers have no mechanism to find relevant matches across the full network. A recommendation system can proactively surface employer-graduate matches based on graduate skills, employer needs, and campus program strengths — leveraging the combined graduate base rather than leaving each campus to compete independently for employer attention.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A talent-matching platform that recommends relevant graduates to registered employers based on skills and program alignment, operating across all campuses and positioning the network's combined graduate base as a differentiated sourcing advantage.

matches.match_rationale: The network lacks intelligence on where its employer relationships stand relative to competitors and where employer perception gaps exist. Research agents can autonomously gather competitive intelligence — scraping job boards, employer career pages, and industry reports — to benchmark the network's employer recognition against peer institutions and identify which sectors are underserved by current campus relationships. This gives leadership evidence-based answers to the recognition problem.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An autonomous competitive intelligence agent that monitors employer hiring patterns, competitor institution employer partnerships, and graduate job board presence to surface actionable gaps in the network's employer engagement strategy.

matches.match_rationale: Employer relationship management across campuses is fragmented because tracking and follow-up is manual and campus-siloed. Task automation agents can orchestrate employer outreach workflows — tracking touchpoints across campuses, triggering follow-up communications, updating a shared CRM, and routing employer inquiries to the right campus contact — without requiring a dedicated central team. This directly addresses the structural gap of each campus building separate employer relationships.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A network-level employer engagement automation system that centralizes employer contact tracking, routes inbound inquiries across campuses, and automates relationship maintenance workflows — functioning as a lightweight shared career services operation.
